titleInfo.title: Sichuan moya shike zhong de Emituofo yu wushi’er pusa
四川摩崖石刻中的阿彌陀佛與五十二菩薩
Buddha Amitābha Surrounded by 52 Bodhisattvas Carved on Polished Cliffs in Sichuan

mods.abstract:
Lei Yuhua distinguishes four types of representations of Amitābha’s Pure Land: Type A consists of Buddha Amitābha and two standing bodhisattvas, sometimes with additional disciples, heavenly kings, and guardians (盛唐, Guangyuan 广元 cave 89).
Type B represents Buddha Amitābha with two bodhisattvas standing or sitting, surrounded by many smaller bodhisattva figures sitting on lotuses (Bazhong 巴中 southern caves 33, 62, western caves 37; also in Tongjiang 通江, Wangcang 旺苍, and Zitong 梓潼. Zhenguan to Kaiyuan eras, 627-741).
Type C has two subtypes: C1 has Buddha Amitābha and two bodhisattvas with heavenly buildings to the left and right from the middle of the 8th century (Bazhong western cave 53); C2 is the fully developed Western Paradise with heavenly architecture, trees, lotus pond, birds, musical instruments, and the 16 visualizations of queen Vaidehi from the middle of the 8th century to the Southern Song dynasty (Dazu 大足 Beishan 北山 Fowan 佛湾 cave 245, Baoding 宝顶 Dafowan 大佛湾 cave 18).
On type B: The earliest sculptures of this type are found in Guangyuan Qianfoyan 千佛崖 and Huangzesi 皇泽寺 from the late Northern Dynasties. They also show the influence of the Southern Dynasties (Chengdu). During the Tang dynasty, this motif flourished in Guangyuan, and also in Bazhong, in a particular large scale. These two sites are situated along two important routes linking Sichuan to Chang’an and Luoyang; the 金牛道 and 米仓道. The 金牛道 connected Chang’an with Sichuan; the 米仓道 the Central Plain with Bazhong. Therefore it is assumed that Sichuan received the impact of Chang’an and Luoyang, but it is so far unclear which influences came from Chang’an, which from Luoyang. In Longmen, more than 71% of all images represent Amitābha and bodhisattva Guanyin. In Longmen, there is one representation of Amitābha with 52 bodhisattvas which dates to the era Gaozong (ca. 660-683) [she doesn’t say where exactly in Longmen; should be Wanfodong]. There are a few differences between these representations in Longmen and those in Sichuan. Some features of the Longmen representations that show up there since the Kaiyuan era are found earlier in Sichuan. Therefore, Lei concludes that the Sichuan examples are not influenced by Longmen [she does not consider that vice versa, Sichuan might have influenced Longmen], but rather by Chang’an. For evidence she relates to Binxian Dafosi, where some examples of lotus thrones interconnected by stems can be found [she probably relates to Qianfodong, but these also date later than the Sichuan examples]. Another characteristic of the Sichuan sculptures are the extremely reluctant jewellery; this points more to Chang’an than to the richly embellished bodhisattva statues of Luoyang. After the Kaiyuan era, representations of the Western Pure Land in Sichuan belong to type C.
